content:
Key Differences, Use Cases, and Best Practices

7 min read
Just now

--

Hey everyone! Today, we’re going to explore the differences between two important Git commands: git fetch and git pull. You will see that these commands may seem to do the same thing, but they serve different purposes and are used in different situations. In this article, we'll learn the differences between them, when to use each command, and some recommended best practices.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Git Fetch vs Git PullFrom those names, you might assume that both commands simply retrieve data from remote repositories. While that’s partially true, each command has a unique purpose and works differently depending on the context. Let’s take a closer look at each command with practical examples.
What is Git Fetch?
git fetch is a Git command that retrieves the latest changes from a remote repository and downloads them to your local repository. The important thing to understand is that it does not modify your current working branch or working directory.
Instead, Git stores the downloaded changes in remote-tracking branches, allowing you to review the updates before deciding whether to merge them into your local branch. If you want to apply those changes, you can later use commands such as git merge or git rebase.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Git Fetchgit fetch [options] [<repository> [<refspec>...]]Key Features of Git Fetch

• Downloads the latest changes from the remote repository.

• Does not modify your current working directory or local branch.

• Updates only the remote-tracking branches.

• Allows you to review incoming changes before integrating them.

• Provides greater control over when and how remote changes are merged.
When Should You Use Git Fetch?
You should use git fetch When you want to check for updates from the remote repository without immediately applying them to your branch.
Some common use cases include:

• Reviewing remote changes before merging them into your local branch.

• Keeping your local repository up to date while maintaining a stable working environment.

• Inspecting updates made by other team members before integrating them into your work.

• Avoiding unexpected merge conflicts by understanding incoming changes in advance.
In short, git fetch is the safer option when you want visibility into remote updates before bringing them into your current branch.
How to Use Git Fetch
Let’s understand how git fetch it works with a simple example.
Step 1: Create a Repository and Push Initial Changes
First, create a file named demo.txt and add the content "Hello Geeks". Then initialize a Git repository, commit the file, and push it to a remote repository.
git init
git add demo.txt
git commit -m "Initial commit"
git remote add origin <repository-url>
git push origin <branch-name>At this point, the demo.txt file exists in both the local and remote repositories. Since we have just pushed our changes, both repositories are synchronized and contain the same commit history.
Step 2: Make Changes in the Remote Repository
Next, let’s assume another change is made directly in the remote repository. For example, demo.txt is updated and a new commit is pushed.
Now, the remote repository contains two commits, while our local repository still contains only the initial commit.
Step 3: Fetch the Latest Changes
Before fetching, you can use the following command to view the commit history in your local repository:
git log --onelineNow run:
git fetchGit will connect to the remote repository and download the latest changes. However, these changes will not be merged into your current branch.
If you check the output, you’ll notice that Git has detected the new commit from the remote repository and updated the remote-tracking branch accordingly.
You can also compare your local branch with the remote-tracking branch to review the incoming changes before applying them.
Step 4: Merge the Fetched Changes
Once you have reviewed the changes and are ready to bring them into your local branch, run:
git merge origin/<branch-name>This merges the fetched changes into your current branch.
To verify the result, check the commit history again:
git log --onelineYou will now see the new commit from the remote repository in your local commit history.

This demonstrates how git fetch Works: it first downloads remote changes without modifying your current branch, allowing you to review them before performing a merge.
What is Git Pull?
git pull is a Git command used to download the latest changes from a remote repository and immediately apply them to your current local branch. Unlikegit fetch, which only downloads changes, git pull automatically integrates those changes into your working branch.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Git PullIn simple terms, git pull is a combination of:
git fetch
git mergeor, depending on your Git configuration:
git fetch
git rebaseBecause the changes are applied automatically, git pull can sometimes result in merge conflicts if the same files have been modified both locally and remotely.
Syntax
git pull [options] [<repository> [<refspec>...]]Key Features of Git Pull

• Downloads the latest changes from the remote repository

• Automatically merges or rebases the changes into your current branch.

• Update your local branch in a single command.

• Helps keep your branch synchronized with the remote repository.

• May generate merge conflicts when local and remote changes overlap.
When Should You Use Git Pull?
You should use git pull When you want to quickly update your local branch with the latest changes from the remote repository.
Some common use cases include:

• Synchronize your branch before starting new development work.

• Pulling updates from teammates in a collaborative project.

• Updating feature branches that are frequently synchronized with the remote repository.

• Quickly bring your local repository up to date when you do not need to inspect incoming changes first.
Since git pull Automatically merges or rebases remote changes into your current branch. It is generally recommended to commit or stash your local changes before running the command. This ensures that your work is safely saved and makes it easier to resolve any merge conflicts that may arise.
As a best practice, use git pull when:

• Your working directory is clean (no uncommitted changes).

• You want to quickly synchronize your branch with the remote repository.

• You do not need to review incoming changes before integrating them.
If you want to inspect remote changes first, consider using git fetch followed by a manual git merge or git rebase.
In short, git pull is the faster and more convenient option when you want remote changes to be downloaded and integrated into your local branch in a single step.
How to Use Git Pull
Now let’s see how git pull works in practice.
Step 1: Make Changes in the Remote Repository
Assume that demo.txt is updated again in the remote repository, and the changes are committed and pushed.
At this point:

• The remote repository contains three commits.

• The local repository contains two commits.
This means the remote repository is ahead of the local repository by one commit.
Step 2: Pull the Latest Changes
To bring the latest changes from the remote repository into your local branch, run:
git pull origin <branch-name>Git will automatically:

• Fetch the latest changes from the remote repository.

• Merge (or rebase, depending on your configuration) those changes into your current branch.
Unlike git fetch, which only downloads the changes, git pull Performs both operations in a single command.
You can think of it as:
git pull = git fetch + git mergeStep 3: Verify the Changes
After the pull operation completes successfully, open demo.txt or check the commit history:
git log --onelineYou will see the latest commit from the remote repository in your local repository.
Result
The changes from the remote repository have now been downloaded and integrated into your local branch. Both repositories contain the same commit history and are synchronized once again.
This is why git pull is a convenient option when you want to quickly update your local branch without manually running separate commands fetch and merge commands.
Difference between git fetch and git pull
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Conclusion
Both git fetch and git pull are essential Git commands used to keep your local repository up to date with changes from a remote repository. The key difference lies in how those changes are handled.
git fetch Downloads the latest changes without modifying your current branch, allowing you to review and inspect updates before integrating them. This makes it a safer option when working on important changes or collaborating with a team.
On the other hand, git pull downloads and immediately integrates remote changes into your current branch, making it a convenient choice when you want to synchronize your work with the remote repository quickly.
As a best practice, consider using git fetch when you need greater control over incoming changes and git pull when your branch is in a clean state, and you are ready to incorporate the latest updates.
Understanding when to use each command will help you work more efficiently, maintain a cleaner Git history, and reduce the likelihood of unexpected merge conflicts.
